supporting_evidence: Statistically most common cause of fever with rash in children aged 1-5 years
Enteroviral, adenoviral, and HHV-6 infections are seasonal and highly prevalent in this age group
Acute-onset maculopapular rash with concurrent fever is the classic viral exanthem pattern
Lethargy and anorexia are expected with febrile viral illness but do not exclude more serious causes
Fever + acute-onset rash + lethargy is the classic early presentation of meningococcemia
HR 185 exceeds fever-adjusted tachycardia — may represent compensatory response to sepsis
BP 85/50 is at or below the 5th percentile for age 4, consistent with early shock
Meningococcal rash can initially appear as blanching erythema before evolving to petechiae/purpura within hours
Children under 5 are at peak risk for invasive meningococcal disease
Case fatality rate 10-15% even with treatment; approaches 100% if missed
Fever with sandpaper-textured rash in preschool child is classic scarlet fever
Peak incidence at age 4-8 years matches this patient
High fever (39.8°C) is consistent with GAS pharyngitis/scarlet fever
Requires rapid strep test — treatment prevents rheumatic fever and reduces transmission
Age 4 is within the classic range (6 months to 5 years, peak 18-24 months)
Fever with rash fulfils 2 of 5 diagnostic criteria
Incomplete Kawasaki disease can present with fewer criteria and is frequently missed
Must be considered if fever continues beyond 5 days — coronary artery aneurysm risk
Early IVIG administration reduces coronary complications from 25% to <5%
Must inquire about recent medication use, including over-the-counter antipyretics
Drug-induced rash can present with concurrent fever
Patient has mild asthma with salbutamol — verify if any recent antibiotics prescribed for respiratory symptoms
Morbilliform drug eruption typically appears 7-14 days after drug initiation

reasoning: 4-year-old female with fever, acute-onset rash, lethargy, and borderline hemodynamic parameters. While viral exanthem remains statistically most probable, I weight meningococcal disease significantly higher than a purely probabilistic assessment would suggest, because the vital sign pattern (HR 185 exceeding fever adjustment, BP at 5th percentile) and the clinical context (lethargy beyond simple febrile malaise) create a concerning hemodynamic picture. The 'glass test' for non-blanching rash should be performed immediately. Kawasaki disease is flagged as a don't-miss because it presents within the classic age window, and early treatment dramatically alters outcomes — missing it in the first 10 days leads to irreversible coronary artery damage. Scarlet fever is readily treatable once identified. Drug reaction should be excluded by history. The key clinical decision is whether this child's hemodynamic profile reflects benign fever-associated tachycardia or early compensated septic shock — this distinction has life-or-death implications and warrants immediate senior review.
